title: Mandatory reporting obligations that institutions routinely ignore
p1:
Many institutions believe silence protects them. Sometimes, the law sees silence as a failure.

A hidden compliance risk is not what an organisation does — it is what it chooses not to report.
p2:
A common misconception is that reporting obligations apply only when there is proven wrongdoing. In reality, many laws require certain institutions to report specific situations, concerns, or information within a defined time.

The surprising part: failing to report can sometimes create liability even when the institution was not directly involved in the original incident.
p3:
Mandatory reporting rules exist because institutions often act as the first line of protection. Schools, financial entities, employers, and other organisations may have duties to escalate concerns instead of handling everything internally.

The practical lesson is simple: a compliance checklist should not only ask, "What actions must we take?" It should also ask, "What must we report, and when?"

Ignoring a reporting duty can turn a manageable issue into a much bigger legal and reputational problem.
conclusion: Do you think institutions ignore mandatory reporting duties because of lack of awareness, fear of consequences, or a desire to avoid scrutiny?
